title: Ultra-freeze media
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"7cudf","text":"This is a recipe for an \"ultra-freeze\" meda recipe. Taken stock from Gibson et al. This recipe enables the quicker recovery of cells stored as a glycerol stock at -80. Cells revived when frozen in this medium seem to revive quicker, especially when inoculated into minimal media. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":76,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dgl6k","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3hckt","text":"A bit onerous to prepare when compared to the standard 15%v/V glyceorl stock approach but can cut up to 24hrs in revival time, very valuable when dealing with slow growing undomesticated microorganisms. steps: Mix Together the following reagents 3 g Tryptone Soya broth 0.5 g Glucose 2 g Skim milk powder 4 g Glycerol
Solution should be turbid, heterogeneous. Store at RT

The paper suggests growing up a lawn but I have found 5mL of liquid culture at stationary phase of chosen microbe works just as well. Just need lots of stationary phase cell material to resuspend in ultra-freeze media for storage. Inoculate overnight culture in media volume that will yield about similar wet cell mass of 5mL of Escherichia coli .

If using a lawn of microbes... scrape clean plate of solid media containing lawn of microbe monoculture. Resuspend goop in 1mL of ultrafreeze media, preferabbly in a labeled cryovial. If using stationary phaes culture. Pellet cells, spin down at 5000rcf for 10 minutes. Pipette off supernatant and resuspend pellett in 1mL of ultrafreeze media, preferrably in a labeled cryovial.
Freeze! Store cryovial with ultra-freeze resuspension in -80.

Scrape a little bit of ice from cryobial. Inoculate onto solid or liquid media and wait for growth. In my experience the pellett is pretty hard. It may be worthwhile to modify the recipe to 18.75g for more vitrious ice. However, 4g glyceorl in a 100mL solution, does not seem to be particularly pernicious to the microbes.
creator: David Shis
